name: The blind men at Jericho
notes: A threefold synoptic parallel of the healing of the blind at Jericho, recorded by Matthew, Mark, and Luke. The shared core is one and the same: the blind sitting by the way side begging, the cry "Jesus, thou Son of David, have mercy," the crowd's rebuke met with crying the more, Jesus standing still to call them, the question "What wilt thou that I should do?", the plea to receive sight, and the immediate healing followed by following him and glorifying God. Matthew records two blind men where Mark and Luke single out one, Mark naming him Bartimaeus the son of Timaeus; this is a complementary vantage, the others lifting up the named man while Matthew counts both. Matthew and Mark place the healing as he departed from Jericho while Luke sets it as he came nigh unto Jericho, which complementary vantage may rest in the old and new sites of the city along one journey through it. Both points are left as a matter for further study; the accounts together magnify the one work of mercy of the Son of David.
